author: @ckimsey77
text:
I'm sure the answer is yes....but have you ever played around much with using nitrated cellulose in the mid-molecular weight range? 

 I know batteries and capacitors are completely separate beasts, but doing R&D on micro-Li batteries printing an extremely thin coat of Li slurry on the collector without it cracking and having minimal surface roughness is extremely difficult.  A low (I think like 3-5% MW if I remember correctly) all but eliminated cracking upon drying, obviously greatly improving properties.  It also eliminated the need for several somewhat harsh solvents

author: @Organiqintel
text: This video was the spark which ignited a raging fire. (Not literally) I have watched it and shown it to so many friends. My obsession with building supercapacitors marked the start of a very wonderfully strange chapter in my life. the one thing that I was inspired to try was to use “neverwet.” As a separator on carbon fiber veil that has been lightly electroplated with either Au, Cu, Ni or Ag. using sodium silicate as both a binder, and electrolyte; when mixed with a very small amount of grapheme oxide and acetone and doing what could be interpreted as a supercritical drying process, by freezing it, and subsequently baking it @ 250-450f in a (homemade) autoclave under the highest vacuum I could generate with a couple of reversed air compressors and a refrigerator pump , the whole thing puffs up like breakfast cereal, and hardens. Technically; I believe it falls under the definition of solid state but I don’t have any collegiate education, so  its highly likely I’m wrong about that…(I later realized I had made a kind of grapheme/silica aerogel) it was solid state but in regular atmospheric conditions(I live on the rainy side of the big island of Hawaii) it would melt after a few days. I also tried this as a bastard version of the “crystal battery” which has been used in the YouTube community by many, for a long time. This tiny device seemed to charge itself. Unfortunately the majority of my toiling was destroyed in the 2018 LERZ eruption which ate half of my neighborhood, and proceeded to melt a few more including the town of Kapoho, the largest eruption in the recorded history of Hawaiian islands. It also added an additional 18 acres or so to the island after filling in Kapoho bay. The lava was extremely liquid, and at one point it measured almost 4000 degrees Fahrenheit. I have been working as a guide in the ocean and on land for a dozen or so years taking tourists to get on top of and adjacent to active lava-ocean entries. I have never measured lava more than about 2700f. My point is that there’s an awful lot of elements that came to the surface in my neighborhood and have been oxidizing in different layers for the past 4 years. The lava was 120ft thick in some places(will be cooling off for the next 20 years or more)but it is porous and has seawater leaching into it. I’m wondering if the flow fields may possibly be able to hold a charge…there’s a geothermal power plant that just so happens to be surrounded by the new lava… I find it very intriguing. I have 2 children who are 3 and 5; I rarely get the opportunity to talk to anyone who has the slightest idea what I am trying to say…what do you think?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: @davidmayhall6567 I actually came here to learn about supercapacitors, so I don't know everything, but I know that graphene is not a dielectric. It conducts quite well. Dielectric basically means an insulator. Conventional capacitors use a dielectric substance, often a ceramic, between the plates that has a higher resistance than air. That allows a higher voltage to build up before you get sparks jumping the gap.

replies.author: @davidmayhall6567
replies.text: @DFPercush yes there is. Ceramic capicitors, and electrolytic capacitors. They have poles   *. And. -. They are made up from 2 plates usually a aluminum foil and they have a paper insulator and a dielectric oil insular instead of a electrolyte. The caps are formed when polarized, making the poles +. -,  and oxidizing the plates slightly this oxide layer serves as a polarized current collector. The capacitor will heat up and explode it current is reversed. There are ac. Capacitors bi polar. Oil based and ceramic. The object of a capacitor is to store electrons. On the plates

replies.author: @Wolf-yp2qk
replies.text: Guitar Guy You can use Polyurethane if it is available as the binder and carrier. But if that is not available you can use some isopropyl alchohol or even acetone as the carrier, and a type of glue for the binder, as I have found that even some epoxys can be used. replies.text: i don't understand what you mean in a circuit the output will meet the load demand - the advantage of a supercp is the power density is so high that it can do stuff a battery can't - the problem is the energy density is low and cost per energy stored is high - otherwise they perform pretty much like a battery anyway in application

replies.author: @reedrichards7719
replies.text: Sure you can do - just use an additional electronic circuit which give you a constant low voltage as output. You might also use a constant current as output - but this is not the problem. The problem for high capacitors is the breaking tension. The higher your working voltage is, the higher the energy amount which can be stored - in the square sense! But - the more isolation distance you put in the device, the less capacity you will obtain. That`s the problem.

author: @Khwartz
text:
¤ CALCULATIONS ¤ >> 8 MWh/m3 << ¤

~°~

4 F/cm * 10,000 cm²/m² = 40,000 F/m² = 40 kF/m²

Let's say 1 mm per set of layer...

40 kF/m² * 1,000 sets of layers per meter = 40,000 kF/m3

40,000 kF/m3 * 0.5 * (1.2 V)² = 28,800 kJ/m3

(28,800 kJ/m3) / (3.6 kJ/Wh) = 8,000 kWh = 8 MWh

8 MWh / (5.5 MWh/1 year of storage/householder ) = ~1.5 year of storage/householder with this very raw set-up!  :D

~°~

Am I Right? or did I miscalculate somewhere? ^^

author: @yuanyuan3056
text:
Hi Robert, I am a PhD student and our lab makes graphene based supercapacitor. Our processes took days therefore I am very impressed by this demo. I tried to repeat this experiment in the lab however I face several problems and would like to ask your advice. I would be really appreciated to have your advice.
1. We dipped a metal foil into activated carbon power mixed with aceton, but weren't able to get anything dipped. We used alternative binder PVA but the performance was not good.
2. In the video it seems the two electrode glued together after you applied the electrolyte. I couldn't get this done in my experiment either, were you using some  binder in the electrolyte?
I really liked this video and the process is really fast and the quality of the supercapacitor is highly controllable. We could share graphene supercapacitor experience if you are interested, thanks!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +yuan hu you made several errors. you need to match your binder - try other binders. there is no binder in the electrolyte. Lack of adhesion usually indicates a couple of things - your film is too thick - the particles in your film are too large.

replies.author: @yuanyuan3056
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith Hi Robert, I am curious if you encounter any shorting issue. I am not sure where I did wrong but I tried the procedure you showed in the video and it seems the current just flow through the current collector-> activated carbon-> electrolyte (seperator) ->activated carbon -> the other current collector, instead of accumulate charges on the electrode. I put a layer of PVA on the electrode after dip-coating the electrode ( with activated carbon+aceton) and let it dried, then I make the device to avoid shorting issue. This works but I only get 0.2F capacitance, not anywhere closer to power a propeller :), could you let me know if you have any suggestions? Thanks!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +yuan hu it will short if you don't use enough binder - the material is nanoscale so easily migrates through the separator if not bound to the electrode

replies.author: @yuanyuan3056
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith Thanks for your reply! I wonder if too much the electrolyte (I used 1M/L NaSO4) could short the capacitor, in the video I saw you used only one drop of the electrolyte. Electrically once the electrolyte touch the current collector shorting will happen, is that correct? Thanks!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +yuan hu no mate - the electrolyte is ionically conductive but not electrically conductive if you make it electrically conductive it will short

author: @flomojo2u
text: Hi, very interesting work, I have watched some of your other videos beforehand but this one was a little tough to follow partly because we didn't get a close up view of the entire process; as it was we had to more or less imagine what was going on several feet away, at the table.  It was also a little tough to tell what was going on because you listed so many possibilities for experimentation without spending some time explaining why you chose the ingredients used in the video.  I understand that we are being encouraged to try things on our own, yet as another person commented it would so helpful to have the complete process documented (Maybe in the video description?) so we have a baseline to compare and try variations on with a known-working model.  It doesn't need to be the best or even fairly good, just something to start with.  Keep up the great work!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +flomojo2u Hiya mate - i am glad you like the video -  I don't really believe in hand holding too much - there has to be work, effort and failure on your side - that's how you learn

replies.author: @flomojo2u
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith I don't really see it as hand holding at all, this is how scientific research is done.  You've made some very impressive claims about the capacity and nature of the super caps you've produced, but to allow anyone -scientific researcher or just a hobbyist- to validate your claims it's necessary to detail the exact steps taken.  The more ground-breaking and revolutionary the claim, the more the need to validate it.  I understand you're encouraging experimentation, however it may discourage some people to be unable to start from a known-good point where they have at least some small hope of it working.  It's not always necessary to re-invent the wheel before improving it.

replies.author: @yougeo
replies.text: I want to say i liked that you explained the different possible effects of all the different possible materials. It made it more clear what was going on with he process not less as this commenter seems to think. Larfe surface area bound but not insulated by the minimum needed adhesive making contact with the conducting plates best suited for your chosen electrolyte. Simple!

replies.author: @flomojo2u
replies.text: +yougeo Well I'm glad someone found it simple ;). Part of my objection to the lists of all the potential materials/electrolytes/binders is that Robert went on to list so many without actually saying what sort of reasons you might want to use a particular electrolyte, for example.  I get that you don't want to use light metals with a strong base/acid as this would dissolve the electrode/plate, that's quite clear.  I just would have appreciated reasons that you *would* want to use various materials together, maybe some of the experiences he's had that would be both interesting and informative.   And as I've mentioned before a simple statement at the video of what we'll be seeing today (I.e. "Today we'll be comparing XX and XX, using XX and XX  Here's why...").

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +flomojo2u This video isn't about making claims mate - it's about encouraging people to experiment - if you feel it discourages you - well - no problem - you can't make everyone happy. From the feedback I get a lot of people are encouraged to try and as far as I am concerned the objective is achieved

It's important to realize that despite you always encourage people to experiment on their own, most want to start with some actually well-working design. So instead of reinventing a wheel, it's obvious that we want to improve something already decent instead of reinventing the whole thing from scratch.

author: @raywharton9425
text:
Thank you for this video! It is exactly what I have been hoping for in the context making carbon based energy storage. Right now I am puttering with making graphene, and I think I have a couple grams of it, but it's still a little short of being in a usable form. My research in this stuff in forced to take a back seat, right now I am putting together a farm business and that takes most of my mental focus. 

I hope to be able to power an electric bicycle with a much larger unit in a couple years, after my LiPoly expires. I think it would be great fun to see one of your large super caps on a bike, that would be a real ground breaking video.

I am curious how great the difference in thickness of active material is between your two systems, and also I wonder what the upper limits are on how thick one can make the active material, and what limiting factors tend to come in to play as one thickens the active material.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Ray Wharton shame about the delay mate - but good luck with the farm. I was thinking about a bike myself - i might buy one to do just exactly that with lol - I should measure it but it was just a how to make it demo. The limiting factors would be resistance mate - the thicker it gets the greater the distance to the collector plate and the higher the resistance. Attachment will be a factor too and electrolyte penetration

replies.author: @BluesDoctor
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith lol. I was sitting here pondering the transient punch through V, Q and resistance issues as I saw this post. Downloaded Grafoil engineering guide and thought, quite an engineering trade-space. Permutations offers much room for optimization and plenty of interesting lab work. Amazing what 50 years of quality lab work can yield for things like grafoil.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +BluesDoctor i tend to recalander the foil down to a few microns via a two roll mill - it is amazing stuff and you are quite right there is a ton of work that can be done here

author: @Inspironator
text: Wow, that was awesome!  I am an engineer rather than a scientist.  Thanks for the explanation -- very interesting.  If the capacitor was pressed properly in a production environment, how much capacitance would result?  Thank you.  I'm looking forward to the next in the series.  I do have one question though, I don't understand what is a symmetric capacitor vs non-symetric.  Is it what I might call a polalrized vs non-polarized capacitor?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Inspironator i have made the 1 cm square graphene one at 9 farads with three dips and compression. Symmetric is just where the same active material is used on both sides - initially it has no polarity - after the first charge discharge cycle it gains polarity - asymmetric is where two different active materials are used - this has polarity but it depends on the material used
